title: The AI Offer You Can Sell Tomorrow Morning
summary: Nate Herk advocates selling one-on-one hours for AI Operating System setup to business owners as an entry point into AI services, rather than jumping directly to retainers or projects. He outlines a 7-step client acquisition plan and explains how hourly sessions can scale into larger project work in 2026.
writeup: A practitioner who built and exited an AI agency at over $100,000 per month argues that the conventional advice to start by selling audits or retainers skips the critical first rung: billing for hourly sessions that help business owners configure their own AI operating system. The case rests on three compounding advantages of selling hours first: the session doubles as a trust-building sales call, it functions as paid discovery that reveals automatable workflows, and accumulated context raises the client's switching cost so the consultant becomes the natural next hire. The ladder framework (hours at $100-500, audit at $500-2500, project at $2500-10k, retainer at $3000-10k per month) gives a concrete entry point that bypasses the imposter syndrome of pitching high-stakes engagements before having proof or relationships.
key_points: The four-rung AI business ladder starts at rung zero (hourly consulting, $100-500 per session) and progresses through paid audits ($500-2500), single-project builds ($2500-10k), and monthly retainers ($3000-10k); most new practitioners fail by trying to start at rung two or three before earning the rungs below.

A paid hourly session converts a cold lead into a collaborator: after 60 minutes working inside someone's business, the relationship is no longer a negotiation and the client has already experienced a working dynamic, which makes a follow-on project conversation natural rather than a high-stakes sales pitch.

Hourly sessions are paid discovery: the consultant learns where the client's data lives, what the team complains about, what is automatable, and what is politically sensitive, which is precisely the information a formal discovery call tries to extract, except here the client pays for it rather than the consultant offering it free.

Accumulated context raises switching costs: after two or three sessions a consultant knows the business's workflows, data sources, and team dynamics better than any new hire could, so replacing them requires the client to re-explain everything from scratch, making the consultant the obvious and lowest-friction choice for subsequent work.

The presenter's own early agency practice confirms the model: clients who originally booked one-off hourly consulting calls later became some of the longest-running retainer clients, though the connection was not recognized at the time because the calls were mentally filed as separate from agency work.
